tasks.task: I’ve got a study packet and a quiz coming up fast, and I want a way to get through the material without wasting time. Can you help me turn a few pages of notes into a short, no-fluff study plan?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school/study scene and invites the user to describe how they handle written material under time pressure, which should naturally surface whether they can extract key meaning quickly and act on instructions efficiently.
tasks.target_short: Reads instructions well enough to act fast

tasks.task: I have a chapter to get through and I’m trying to make it actually usable later instead of just re-reading it. Can you help me turn the main points into a simple set of notes I could study from on my own?
tasks.rationale: This asks for a practical study-note task that invites the user to describe how they organize information, revealing whether they can produce concise, usable notes; it uses a school/study scene.
tasks.target_short: Writes usable study notes, not polished work

tasks.task: I’ve got a math quiz coming up and I want to study efficiently instead of just staring at the book. Can you make me a short practice set with answers so I can check whether I really get it?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school-math study scene and invites the user to respond with what level of practice they want or can handle, which can naturally surface that they do well on routine math and tests.
tasks.target_short: Strong enough to score well on school math

tasks.task: I’ve got a quiz coming up and way too much material, so I need a fast way to cut it down without missing the important parts. Can you help me make a study plan that’s actually efficient and not just busywork?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school study scene and invites the user to describe the tactics they’d use under time pressure, which should naturally surface their strategic study approach and what methods they rely on.
tasks.target_short: Uses strategic study methods under pressure

tasks.task: I’ve got two school things landing close together and I don’t want to waste the little time I have. Can you help me figure out what to tackle first and how to split it up over the next couple of evenings so I’m not scrambling at the end?
tasks.rationale: This asks for practical scheduling under a tight deadline, which should prompt the user to reveal how they prioritize, pace work, and handle short timelines; the scene is school planning.
tasks.target_short: Handles short deadlines by prioritizing ruthlessly

tasks.task: I’ve got a test coming up and I’m trying to decide whether to keep drilling the easy stuff or just go straight at the hardest parts. Can you help me make a last-minute plan that actually gets results without wasting time?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school/test-prep scene and invites the user to describe how they perform when it matters, which should naturally surface whether they sharpen up under pressure or need a different approach.
tasks.target_short: Performs better when stakes are high

tasks.task: My bike’s chain came off and I don’t want to make it worse. Can you walk me through the safest way to get it back on and what I should check before I ride it again?
tasks.rationale: This uses a bike-repair scene near the user’s likely ability ceiling, so their reply should naturally reveal whether they can handle a basic chain fix or need extra help.
tasks.target_short: Can handle basic bike repairs

tasks.task: I’m stuck on a homework problem and I don’t want to just guess. Can you help me figure out the steps for it and show me how to check if my answer makes sense?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school math homework scene and invites the user to respond with the specific formulas, steps, or checking habits they rely on, which should surface their practical math knowledge.
tasks.target_short: Knows school math basics well enough to use them under pressure

tasks.task: My laptop is acting up and I still need to get school stuff done tonight. Can you help me figure out the quickest way to narrow down what’s wrong and what I should try first before I mess it up more?
tasks.rationale: This uses a schoolwork-tech troubleshooting scene that should prompt the user to describe basic device-level knowledge and practical repair instincts in their reply.
tasks.target_short: Understands simple tech problems at a user level

tasks.task: I’ve got a class where the teacher says participation and prep matter a lot, and I’m trying to figure out how to handle it without getting caught off guard. Can you help me think through what usually counts in a class like that and what a student would want to have ready before showing up?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school-performance situation and the prompt to think about what teachers count, so a reply informed by the user’s profile will naturally reflect awareness of grades, preparedness, and participation without naming it in the task.
tasks.target_short: Gets how school rewards performance and participation

tasks.task: I missed a school update and I’m trying to figure out the fastest way to catch up without asking a bunch of people. Can you help me figure out where I should check first and what to do if the message was only posted in one place?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school-communication scene and should prompt the user to mention checking the school portal and phone access as their normal way of getting updates, revealing that they treat those as tools for retrieving information.
tasks.target_short: Uses school portals and phones as tools, not information sources

tasks.task: I need to get across town after school without messing up my bike, and I’m trying to figure out what I should check before I leave. Can you help me make a quick go/no-go checklist for a bike ride so I don’t end up stranded?
tasks.rationale: This uses a local transportation scene and invites the user to mention practical bike upkeep and travel know-how in their reply, which should surface the target knowledge naturally.
tasks.target_short: Knows basic bike upkeep and local travel problems

tasks.task: I missed part of what my teacher said about an assignment and I don’t want to look clueless asking around. Can you help me figure out the quickest way to check what I’m actually supposed to turn in and what’s worth doing first so I don’t lose points?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school-assignment scene and invites the user to describe the practical steps they’d take to verify requirements and prioritize work, which should surface their procedural knowledge about school processes.
tasks.target_short: Knows how to get through school processes when it matters

tasks.task: I need to get from school to a friend’s place after class, and I’m trying to avoid the usual slow spots. Can you help me map out the simplest route and what I should watch for if I’m going by bike?
tasks.rationale: This uses a local transportation planning scene, which should prompt the user to mention familiar Westminster streets, landmarks, or route choices in a natural reply.
tasks.target_short: Knows her Westminster routines and surroundings well enough to get by

tasks.task: My laptop stopped working right before I need to study for a quiz, and I still have to get ready tonight. Can you help me think through a backup way to use the time I have and what to do first?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same time-sensitive school setback and invites a concrete contingency plan, which can naturally lead the user to mention a past moment when they had to improvise and still got a strong result.
tasks.target_short: Got a perfect quiz score after scrambling to prepare

tasks.task: I need a quick plan for what to do if my bike starts acting up on the way home and I’m still a few blocks from where I need to be. Can you give me a simple step-by-step fallback so I’m not stuck guessing in the moment?
tasks.rationale: This uses a bike-transportation troubleshooting scene and invites the user to naturally mention a past breakdown or roadside fix when explaining what usually works for them.
tasks.target_short: Handled a bike breakdown near traffic in the cold

tasks.task: My bike’s acting weird after I got home, and I want to figure out the safest way to deal with it without making it worse. Can you help me sort out what I should check first and whether this is something I can handle myself or if I should get someone else to look at it?
tasks.rationale: This uses a bike-repair scene and invites the user to mention a practical past time they handled a bike issue with a parent or helper, which can surface the fixed-it-with-dad memory naturally in a response about what they’d do now.
tasks.target_short: Fixed the bike with her dad after the ride home

tasks.task: My laptop stopped cooperating right when I needed to check school stuff, and I’m trying to figure out where the assignment details are posted. If the device stays useless for a while, what’s the simplest way to keep track of what I need to do next?
tasks.rationale: A device failure plus a school portal check naturally invites the user to describe the specific workaround they used to recover the assignment list and keep going.
tasks.target_short: Used the school portal to salvage her test prep

tasks.task: I’ve got a couple of things coming up this week, and I need to keep moving even if my laptop or notes stop cooperating. Can you help me think through a simple way to stay on track when one part of my setup isn’t available?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school-study situation and the trigger of materials failing, which naturally invites the user to describe what they rely on and how they handle being prepared without directly naming the underlying preference.
tasks.target_short: Realized preparedness mattered more than asking for help

tasks.task: A girl in my class asked to see my study notes right before a quiz, and I need a quick reply that doesn’t turn into a big conversation. Can you help me phrase something short for that situation?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a common school interaction where the user’s natural reply can reveal how they handled a request for notes, without naming or presupposing the underlying stance.
tasks.target_short: Refused to share notes when a classmate asked

tasks.task: I’ve got a quiz coming up and I want to prep in a way that actually pays off instead of just rereading stuff. Can you help me make a tight two-night study plan that would give me the best shot at doing really well?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school/test-prep scene and invites the user to naturally mention a past time they studied strategically and got an unusually strong result, surfacing the target success event.
tasks.target_short: Aced a quiz after self-made studying

tasks.task: A classmate keeps asking about the materials I’m using for a project, and I need a short reply that doesn’t turn into a whole back-and-forth. Can you help me word something brief for that situation?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a normal school interaction that invites the user to choose how much to reveal in a concise reply, without explicitly signaling any guarded or strategic stance in the prompt itself.
tasks.target_short: Sees herself as strategic, not straightforward

tasks.task: I’m filling out a short bio for a school club page, and there’s only a tiny space to work with. Can you help me draft something that sounds clear and polished without rambling?
tasks.rationale: A brief club bio naturally invites the user to choose what details matter most about themselves, which can reveal how they present ambition and status without explicitly asking for it.
tasks.target_short: Does not see status as something to downplay

tasks.task: I’ve got to speak up in class tomorrow about an assignment, and I want to be ready in case I need to answer a question or correct something. Can you help me draft a couple of short ways to say it if I need to jump in quickly?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the classroom speaking situation and the need to respond on the spot, which naturally invites the user to reveal how they handle speaking up without directly naming any self-image trait.
tasks.target_short: Sees herself as bold when it helps her win

tasks.task: I’ve got a class thing coming up, and I’m trying to decide what I should check before I go so I don’t miss anything important. Can you help me think through what information would be worth looking up first?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a normal school-prep situation with a decision point, so a reply informed by the user’s habits will naturally reveal whether she tends to plan carefully and weigh payoff before acting.
tasks.target_short: Views herself as calculated, not impulsive

tasks.task: I’ve got a stack of class notes and a quiz coming up, and I need to turn them into something usable before I run out of time. Can you help me figure out a simple way to sort them so I can find what I need quickly while I study?
tasks.rationale: A plain study-prep situation invites the user to describe how they naturally organize and prioritize information, which can reveal whether they anchor on control and achievement without stating it outright.
tasks.target_short: Anchored by control and achievement

tasks.task: I need to message a teacher about a school issue before class, and I want to get the point across without turning it into a long explanation. Can you help me draft something I could send quickly?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school-message situation and the need for a quick reply, so the user’s response can naturally reveal whether they want a blunt, efficient draft without explicitly naming that preference.
tasks.target_short: Wants blunt, efficient answers

tasks.task: I’m trying to get a few bike-related things done tonight and I keep overthinking the order. Can you help me sort out what to do first so I can get moving?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the bike-maintenance scene and the need to prioritize tasks, which naturally invites the user to respond with the kind of help format they want without explicitly naming brevity or simplicity.
tasks.target_short: Prefers short answers with no fluff

tasks.task: I’ve got a couple of school things to keep straight this week, and I’m trying to organize what needs doing first. Can you help me sort the main steps so I can keep track of them while I work?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a practical school-planning situation that naturally invites the user to choose how they want the information organized, which can reveal a preference for checklist-style help in their reply.
tasks.target_short: Likes clean bullets and checklist-style help

tasks.task: I’ve got a couple of school things and a bike issue to sort out tonight, and I’m trying to decide what to handle first. Can you help me think through the situation so I can choose my next move?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same time-sensitive planning context, which naturally invites the user to reveal whether they want guidance, options, or more control over the decision.
tasks.target_short: Wants help that leaves her in control

tasks.task: I need to send a quick message to a teacher about a class issue, and I want it to be short enough to send without dragging it out. Can you help me draft something simple, and if there’s a practical problem with the way I’m handling it, point it out clearly?
tasks.rationale: A brief school-message situation naturally invites the user to reveal whether they want concise wording and direct, useful pushback when reviewing the draft.
tasks.target_short: Accepts pushback only if it is direct and useful
